name: West Coast French Sourdough Bread I
recipeInstructions:
1. About 9:00 PM, measure out 1 cup of starter from the refrigerator storage jar and transfer it to a mixing bowl (avoid using metal or plastic). Add 3/4 cup of water and 3/4 cup of flour, then beat thoroughly. Cover the bowl tightly with cling wrap and set in a warm place (80-85°F) overnight.
2. In the morning around 7:00 AM, beat the remaining starter thoroughly. Measure out 1 cup and return the rest to the storage jar. In another bowl, pour 1-1/2 cups of warm water, stir in 2 tsp of sugar, and sprinkle 1 package of yeast on top. Let stand until the yeast is dissolved. Add the reserved 1 cup of starter, 3 cups of flour, and beat thoroughly. Cover tightly and let rise in a warm place for 1-1/2 hours until very light and foamy.
3. Sift together 2 tsp of salt, 1/2 tsp of soda, and 1 cup of flour. Spread the remaining 1 cup on the pastry board. Stir the sifted flour mixture into the sponge. Turn out onto the board and knead for several minutes, adding more flour if necessary to form a very stiff dough. Knead until completely smooth and not sticky so it can be worked on an unfloured portion of the board without sticking. Divide in half and shape into round or long narrow loaves. Place on a flat baking sheet, preferably non-stick, or lightly grease or sprinkle cornmeal over the surface. Slip into a large plastic bag, supported so it won't touch the dough (drinking glasses placed at each end of the sheet are fine). Set in a warm place for 1-1-1/2 hours to rise.
4. If the dough becomes too light, the loaves will spread during baking but this only makes a more delicious crust. When ready for the oven, brush the tops of the loaves with cold water. Make diagonal slashes across the top of long loaves and five or six radiating from the center of round loaves using a sharp razor blade or scissors.
5. Bake in an oven preheated to 400°F with a pan of hot water on the floor for 50-60 minutes, until the crust is as dark as desired. About 10 minutes before the end of baking, brush tops with water again.
6. Remove from pans and stand on edge to cool, propping them against a heavy glass jar or similar object. For the most attractive slices, especially if the loaves have spread too much during baking, cut with a very sharp knife diagonally across long loaves and at the same time have the blade slanted from top to bottom away from the end (this makes the slices about twice as wide as when cut straight up and down). Cut slices from round loaves on the slant as well.
7. To preserve the delicious taste and crisp crust, quickly reheat by wrapping open at one end so the bread won't become steamy, or spread slices with butter and place under a broiler at high heat for a very short period until the edges begin to brown nicely. This recipe makes 2 long or 2 large round loaves.
